plainMeaning: Still bowing to holy towns, the worshipper here salutes the Jina-temples of Dev-pāṭaṇ, Devagiri and Navānagar, and of Diu and every other port.
recitation: devakapāṭaṇ devagiri, sā0 ǁ nave-nagar vandī joy ǀ dīvādik savi bandare, sā0

wordByWord.etymology: deva ('god') + pāṭaṇ < Sanskrit pattana ('town, port-city'); a place-name.
wordByWord.notes: A coastal/Saurāṣṭra town of this name (cf. Dev-pāṭaṇ/Prabhāsa-Pāṭaṇ); the exact identification is not fixed by the book — see notes.
Devagiri = Daulatabad in the Deccan, an old Jaina centre; the list here reaches beyond Gujarat.

wordByWord.gloss: [refrain mark — the dhāla's recurring ṭeka word, abbreviated]
wordByWord.etymology: Abbreviation (the '૦' is the book's truncation mark) standing for a repeated refrain syllable/word.
wordByWord.notes: Kept verbatim per the book's orthography; the full word it abbreviates is not expanded in the text and is left uncertain.

wordByWord.etymology: nava ('new') + nagar < Sanskrit nagara ('city'); 'the new city'.
wordByWord.notes: Read as the Saurāṣṭra port-town Navānagar (Jāmnagar), a noted Jaina centre; a literal reading 'the new city' is also possible.

wordByWord.etymology: Absolutive of √vand ('to salute, venerate'); Prakrit vandi(a), Sanskrit vanditvā.
wordByWord.notes: Folds the gerund 'having bowed' into the chant; pairs with joy below.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati joï/joya < Apabhraṁśa joi < Prakrit joi/diṭṭha < Sanskrit √dṛś ('to see').
wordByWord.notes: 'vandī joy' = bow and behold — darśana and vandana joined; can also read adverbially as 'behold, having bowed!'

wordByWord.etymology: Dīva ('Diu', the island port; cf. Sanskrit dvīpa 'island') + ādika ('and the rest, et cetera').
wordByWord.notes: An ādi-compound: names Diu and implies the other port-shrines not separately listed.

wordByWord.etymology: Apabhraṁśa savi/savve < Prakrit savva < Sanskrit sarva ('all').
wordByWord.notes: Generalizes the salutation beyond the named places.

wordByWord.gloss: at the ports / in the harbour-towns
wordByWord.etymology: Locative of bandar ('port, harbour'), a Persian loan (bandar) widespread in Gujarati maritime usage.
wordByWord.notes: Locative plural sense: 'in all the port-towns', i.e. their Jina-temples.

wordByWord.etymology: Same abbreviated refrain as above, closing the second half-line.
wordByWord.notes: Recurs at each half-line end as the dhāla's ṭeka; kept verbatim.

literalTranslation: Dev-pāṭaṇ, Devagiri — [refrain] — Navānagar: having bowed, behold (them); Diu and all the rest, in every port — [refrain].
idiomaticTranslation: Bow now and gaze in reverence on Dev-pāṭaṇ, on Devagiri, on Navānagar — and on Diu and the shrines of every harbour-town besides; to all of them I do homage.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This couplet keeps the tīrtha-garland moving outward. The previous verse held inland Gujarat (Khambhāt, Tārāpur, Mātar, Gandhār); here the gaze swings to the Saurāṣṭra coast and the Deccan — Dev-pāṭaṇ and Navānagar on the sea, Devagiri (Daulatabad) far inland in the south — before the next verse turns back north to Vaḍnagar, Iḍar and Mālwā. The verse's hinge is the phrase 'vandī joy': the poet binds bowing (vandana) to beholding (darśana), so that naming a place is itself a mental visit to its Jina-temple. The second half then performs the hymn's signature totalizing move: rather than list every coastal shrine, it says 'dīvādik savi bandare' — 'Diu and all the ports' — using the ādi-compound and 'savi' (all) to sweep in every harbour-town's caitya at once. The little word 'savi' does real work: it tells the reciter that the enumeration is illustrative, not exhaustive, and that no Jina-temple, named or unnamed, is left unbowed-to. The repeated 'sā0' refrain stitches this half-verse into the dhāla's melody.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse is bhāva-tīrtha-vandana — venerating pilgrimage shrines in spirit, by image and name, without physical travel. Saluting Diu 'and all ports' enacts the principle that reverence is measured by intention, not by miles covered: the worshipper who can never reach these coasts still earns the merit of the bow. This sustains and steadies samyaktva (right faith), the recurring aim of the tīrthamālā.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: While chanting, let each name surface as a picture of its temple, bow inwardly, and at 'savi bandare' open the bow to every shrine you will never visit. It trains a pilgrimage of the mind and loosens the idea that merit requires arrival.

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā (108 tīrthas) — the Pakkhī tīrtha-litany of the same gaccha
crossReferences.source: Mahendrasinhasūri; Panch Pratikraman Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgaccha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Dhāla 8 tīrthamālā, preceding and following verses (Khambhāt…Gandhār; Vaḍnagar…Mālwā)

sources.title: Daulatabad Fort (originally Devagiri Fort)
sources.consultedFor: Identifying Devagiri as the Deccan hill-fort city (Daulatabad), an old Jaina centre.

sources.title: Diu, India
sources.consultedFor: Identifying Diu (Dīva) as the fortified island port named in the verse.

sources.title: Shri Navanagar Tirth (Jamnagar)
sources.consultedFor: Identifying Navānagar (Jāmnagar) as a Jaina tīrtha town behind 'navenagar'.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Dictionaries (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Roots for the glosses: √vand (vandana), √dṛś (joy/behold), sarva (savi), pattana (pāṭaṇ), dvīpa (Dīva).

notes: Genre and run of place-names are clear; specific identifications carry some uncertainty. 'devakapāṭaṇ' is read as Dev-pāṭaṇ (a Saurāṣṭra/coastal town; possibly Prabhāsa-Pāṭaṇ), but the book does not gloss it. 'navenagar' is taken as Navānagar (Jāmnagar); a literal 'the new city' is grammatically possible. 'sā0' is the book's abbreviation of the dhāla's recurring refrain word, kept verbatim and NOT expanded here because the text does not spell it out — treated as uncertain rather than invented. 'vandī joy' is rendered as 'bow and behold' (absolutive + 'see'); an imperative reading 'behold!' is also tenable. Spelling preserved verbatim per the edition's orthography.
